course_description:
Social work practice is understood in relation to a number of methods. Students will be taught, and are expected to demonstrate, competence in social work practice as that is practiced in communities, and a capacity to integrate several methods required by a particular piece of work. Please note that as well as weekly lectures and tutorials for this course, students will be invited to attend 2-3 peer conversation cafes.

Welcome to SWSP2088: Direct Practice 2

This course builds on your developing knowledge and skills in social work by focusing on practice with groups and communities. We will explore two key methods of social work: group work practice and community work practice , often referred to as mezzo and macro practice. Both these methods focus on social work practice in building and sustaining groups and communities.

In conjunction with SWSP2077, which focused on direct practice with individuals in both voluntary and involuntary casework settings, SWSP2088 aims to facilitate the development of your practice skills and theoretical understandings to enable you to undertake a field placement involving group and community work practice. As with SWSP2077, this course begins by exploring social work practice as a purposeful activity. We consider how we negotiate our practice purpose through our context, our professional base, as well as the values, experiences, skills and perspectives that we bring to social work. This course builds on your existing skills base and introduces competencies for effective social work practice with groups and communities.

Core Knowledge and Concepts

In SWSP2088, we will explore a range of knowledge areas and practice skills:

Direct Practice Skills (Introductory Level)

Acknowledgement

The University of Queensland acknowledges the Traditional Owners and their custodianship of the lands on which the university now stands. We pay our respects to their Ancestors and their descendants, who continue their cultural and spiritual connections to Country. We recognise their valuable contributions to Australia and global society.ﾠﾠ

ASWEAS Required Curriculum Content Areas covered in this course:

The Bachelor of Social Work (Hons) program is accredited by the Australian Association of Social Workers. The Australian Social Work Education and Accreditation Standards (2020) stipulates that accredited social work programs are required to include content that addresses the five core curriculum areas listed below. This course contains content in the following curriculum areas:

1. Constructions of social work purpose, place, and practice

2. Power, oppression, and exploitation

4. Culture, identity, and discrimination

5. Psychosocial health and wellbeing across the lifecycle

The BSocWk(Hons) program will undergo re-accreditation in 2026 under the new ASWEAS (2024), which outlines updated required curriculum. This course content maps to the following:

1. Social worker values and professional identity.

2. Approaches to social work

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Consultations will the Course Coordinator are available
by appointment.
If you would like to arrange a meeting, please contact the course coordinator by email.
Throughout the semester, there will be
2-3 Peer Conversation Cafés
designed to support your learning and course assignments. Dates and times for these session will be announced in advance so please keep an eye on course email communications.
All students are required to
sign up for a tutorial group.
If you need to request a change to your allocated tutorial time, please email the Course Coordinator. While every effort will be made to accommodate requests, reallocation cannot be guaranteed. When considering tutorial changes, the following priorities will apply:
Highest priority will be given to students with care responsibilities that affect their ability to attend their allocated tutorial. In such cases, written verification from relevant services (such as child care providers) confirming that alternative arrangements are not possible will be required.
Requests from full-time students seeking changes due to paid work commitments will be considered, but will receive lower priority.

learning_outcomes.description: Use skills in engaging groups in communities - Identify and apply basic skills in community work to influence and implement social policy through community consultation processes.

Use skills in engaging groups in communities - Identify and apply basic skills in working with groups and communities, including practice skills for engaging, assessment and intervention with communities.
Use skills in engaging groups in communities - Understand and use community education processes.
Use skills in engaging groups in communities - Understand how social work values apply to group and community work practice as methods of professional social work practice.
Use skills in engaging groups in communities - Articulate theoretical frameworks for group and community work practice in professional social work practice.

Use skills in engaging groups in communities - Recognise different forms of community, especially those based on location and those based on cultural identity, and the implications of these differences for promoting inclusive communities.
Use skills in engaging groups in communities - Understand how to contribute to culturally responsive approaches to working with groups and communities.

Use skills in engaging groups in communities - Apply research skills to access key data sources which illuminate community need and capacity for the development of effective community consultation submissions.

assessment_summary.title: Group Workshop and Individual Reflection
assessment_summary.due_date: 22/8/2025 - 12/9/2025; Part 1: The Group Skills Workshops commence in Week 4 and continue until Week 7.; 26/09/2025 1:00 pm; Part 2: The Individual Reflection is due in Week 9 on Friday.

assessment_details.title: Group Workshop and Individual Reflection
assessment_details.due_date: 22/8/2025 - 12/9/2025 Part 1: The Group Skills Workshops commence in Week 4 and continue until Week 7. 26/09/2025 1:00 pm Part 2: The Individual Reflection is due in Week 9 on Friday.
assessment_details.task_description: This description is a brief summary of the assessment task. For full details, including the marking rubric, please refer to the course Learn UQ (Blackboard) site. The assessment consists of two parts, each worth 25% of your final grade for this assessment. Part 1: Group Workshop Weighting: 25% Description: In the tutorials from Week 4 (22 August) to Week 7 (12 September), students will work in groups (4 students) to facilitate one of four community work practice skills workshops. The aim of this group task is to demonstrate the ability to define, analyse and apply direct practice skills in group work and community work. Group Allocations: We will be organising group membership, topics and the presentation schedule in our first tutorial. Please ensure that you attend this class. If you are unable to attend, you will be placed in a group and provided the contact details of your group members. It is your responsibility to make contact with your group as soon as possible following receipt of those details. Tutorial Participation Requirement: If a student is unable to attend and participate in the Week 4 - Week 7 tutorials due to illness or an unexpected personal crisis, they will be required to complete a catch-up group work activity (e.g. facilitating a group activity in a tutorial). This ensures that all students meet the learning outcomes required by Australian Association of Social Workers (AASW) accreditation standards. Part 2: Individual Reflection Weighting: 25% Description: Students will undertake a reflective analysis to deepen their understanding of community work practice. For this task, students will write an (individual) reflection where they critically analyse and apply the principles of community education/problem-posing drawing on their experience of facilitating the group workshop and the experience of participating as a group member in at least one other group workshop. To support their individual reflection, students will have the opportunity in the tutorials to journal their learning experiences following each of the group workshops. These reflective journal entries will support this Part 2 of the assessment. Use of AI: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. While students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools. Please complete the reflective statement on how AI was used in this assessment taking into account ethical considerations. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
This assessment requires students to submit an electronic version of their assignment via Blackboard. The electronic version of your assignment will be submitted via Blackboard, using the Turnitin software.
Please refer to ITS help if you experience difficulties in submitting your assessment in Blackboard or using Turnitin.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extension requests are submitted online via my.UQ. Extension requests received in any other way will not be approved.
Extension guidelines
Extension requests should be submitted prior to the assessment deadline. Requests for extensions received after the assessment item submission due date may not be approved. Late requests must include evidence of the reasons for the late request as per the guidelines in my.UQ . The extension granted should be in proportion to the period of illness or disruption caused by the exceptional circumstances and will typically be no more than the specified maximum extension length for each assessment. A maximum of three assessment requests can be submitted for each assessment, and the third request must include an Assessment Management Plan. Students who have been ill or unable to attend class for more than 4 weeks in a semester are advised to carefully consider whether they are capable of successfully completing their courses and may need to consider applying for removal of course . Students who have a disability or a chronic medical condition (including a mental health condition) are encouraged to meet with a Disability Advisor within Student Services to request a Student Access Plan (SAP) with a clause on extension requests. Students who have an SAP can apply for a 7-day extension.
Outcome of application
Extension requests are generally processed within 2 working days.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Community Development Project
assessment_details.task_description: This description is a brief summary of the task. For full details, including the marking rubric, please refer to the course Learn UQ (Blackboard) site. Description: The aim of the Community Development Project is for students to demonstrate a capacity to design a community project that addresses community need and builds capacity underpinned by community development principles and skills. The project will also meet the course learning objective to demonstrate culturally responsive practice approaches to working with groups and communities. Students will have the opportunity to design a community development project in the form of a funding application. The funding proposal is to be completed on a pre-formatted document. Use of AI: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. While students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools. Please complete the Student Reflective Statement on the Appropriate use of AI cover sheet for this assessment. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Overview and Introduction to the course In this introductory session, we explore group work and community development as methods of social work practice. We examine community as the focus and context of social work practice. Through the course overview, you'll gain an understanding of the course structure, expectations and requirements which will enable you to engage meaningfully with the learning over the next 13 weeks. There are no tutorials this week. Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L06

Community Education Today we focus on the theory and practice of community education. This class will provide the foundation for planning and facilitating your group workshops in Weeks 4-7 of the course. Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L05

Introductory Tutorial In this first tutorial, we introduce ourselves and set group agreements. We organise the groups for the workshops and begin to consider community work practice skills. Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L06

Culturally Responsive Community Work Today we host a guest practitioner who will guide our learning about protocol for respectful engagement with culturally diverse communities. Learning outcomes: L02, L06, L07

Community Education We review community education principles and identify important skills for the group workshops. We will discuss approaches to, and skills in, engaging with culturally diverse communities. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L05, L07

Working with Groups in a Community Context We discuss the types of groups in a community work context. We explore the different ways community workers facilitate group participation. This theme will continue in the tutorial. Learning outcomes: L02, L05

Maximising Group Participation This tutorial begins with the first skills workshop which focuses on achieving inclusive group leadership through maximising participation in groups. Following the workshop we discuss material from the course readings with a focus on the stage theory of groups. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L06

Groups as Vehicles for Change Today we return to the theme of working with groups and consider some practical examples of how community groups are used to achieve change. Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L05, L07

Managing Conflict in Groups The second group workshop focuses on understanding and managing conflict in groups. We discuss conflict as an inevitable part of groups and consider how conflict can be managed constructively. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L07
Asset Based Community Development We explore the principles of an Asset Based Approach to Community Development. Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L05, L07

Assessing Community Needs & Capacities The skills workshop focuses on how to assess a community's needs and capacities. We discuss Asset-Based Community Development (ABCD). Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L05
A Critical Practice Perspective Today we consider forms of community work that seek to faciltate social change. Learning outcomes: L02, L04, L05, L06, L07

Working with the Media The final skills workshop addresses working with the media (print or social media) to represent and advocate for your community. Students practice the skill of preparing media releases in community practice. We consider whether generative AI can play a role. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Community Consultation and Advocacy Today we explore the social worker's role in representing and advocating for/with communities through community consultation processes. Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L06, L07, L08

Community Consultation Processes In this tutorial, we explore community consultation processes and discuss the role of an advocacy paper as a means to represent community. Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L06, L07, L08

Community Work Practice & Policy Processes We continue to explore community consultation processes from a practice perspective. We focus on the social work role and writing an advocacy paper. Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L06, L07, L08

Community Consultation Processes In this tutorial, we continue to explore community consultation processes and begin to discuss and plan your community development project. Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L06, L07, L08

Preparing your Funding Proposal Part 1 We consider how social workers in community practice access funds for community activities. We discuss the following elements of your funding submission for your community development project such as: writing a budget, timeline and milestones. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L06, L07

Preparing your Funding Proposal Part 1 In this tutorial we focus on the skills involved in writing a funding submission. The class will engage in writing exercises. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L06, L07

Preparing your Funding Proposal Part 2 We continue to focus on preparing the funding submission for your community development project and consider all the components. We discuss the community development principles and values underpinning your project design. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L06, L07

Preparing your Funding Proposal Part 2 In this tutorial we continue to focus on the practice skills involved in writing your funding submission. We reflect on the community development theory, principles, values and skills that underpin your project design. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
Implementation, Evaluation and Review Part 1 Today we focus on project evaluation processes to support this important element of your funding submission. Learning outcomes: L04, L05, L06, L07
Implementation, Evaluation and Review Part 1 In this tutorial we discuss project evaluation processes and engage in activities to support this learning. Learning outcomes: L04, L05, L06, L07

Implementation, Evaluation and Review Part 2 In the final lecture, we conclude our discussions of the funding submission and evaluation processes. Key course themes will be reviewed. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08

Course Review In the first half of the tutorial we review your learning in the course. In the last half we celebrate your achievements over the semester - activity to be decided together! Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
